metadata.purpose: Assess prerequisite knowledge before learning about collecting data, tally marks, pictographs, bar graphs, and interpreting data

prerequisiteConcepts.conceptName: Counting objects and sorting them into groups
Using comparison words like more, less, most, and least
Reading numbers and understanding number values up to 100
Adding small numbers to find totals

questions.question: A bag has 3 red balls, 5 blue balls, and 2 green balls. How many balls are there in all?
questions.explanation: Count all the balls: 3 red + 5 blue + 2 green = 10 balls in total.

commonMistakes.mistake: Added only two groups and missed one
commonMistakes.conceptGap: Forgetting to count all the groups
commonMistakes.remediation: Count each group carefully and add them all together

questions.question: Which of these is a way to sort a collection of fruits?
questions.explanation: We can sort fruits by colour (red apples, yellow bananas, green grapes). Sorting means putting similar things together in groups.

questions.question: In a class, 8 children like mango, 6 like apple, and 4 like banana. How many children were asked about their favourite fruit?
questions.explanation: To find the total number of children, add all the groups: 8 + 6 + 4 = 18 children.

commonMistakes.mistake: Added only two groups instead of all three
commonMistakes.conceptGap: Not counting all the categories when finding the total
commonMistakes.remediation: Make sure to add every group when finding the total

questions.question: There are 9 cats and 5 dogs. Which group has more animals?
questions.explanation: 9 cats is more than 5 dogs. Since 9 > 5, the cats group has more animals.

questions.question: Group A has 12 stickers, Group B has 7 stickers, and Group C has 15 stickers. Which group has the most stickers?
questions.explanation: Compare the numbers: 12, 7, and 15. The biggest number is 15, so Group C has the most stickers.

commonMistakes.mistake: Chose the first group listed instead of the largest
commonMistakes.conceptGap: Not comparing all groups to find the greatest value
commonMistakes.remediation: Look at the number for each group and pick the biggest one

questions.question: Riya has 4 pencils and Sam has 4 pencils. What can we say about their pencils?
questions.explanation: Riya has 4 and Sam has 4. Since both numbers are the same, they have an equal number of pencils.

questions.question: What number does the mark point to on a number line that goes 0, 10, 20, 30, 40, 50 if the mark is exactly at the third line?
questions.explanation: The number line goes by 10s: 0 is the first mark, 10 is the second, and 20 is the third mark.

commonMistakes.mistake: Counted the mark as 3 instead of reading the scale
commonMistakes.conceptGap: Not reading the scale of the number line
commonMistakes.remediation: Always check what each mark on the number line stands for before reading a value

questions.question: On a number line marked 0, 5, 10, 15, 20, 25, 30, what is the value halfway between 10 and 20?
questions.explanation: Halfway between 10 and 20 is 15. You can see it marked right on the number line between these two numbers.

commonMistakes.mistake: Guessed a number between 10 and 20 without checking the scale
commonMistakes.conceptGap: Not understanding that halfway means exactly in the middle
commonMistakes.remediation: Find the exact middle by adding both numbers and dividing by 2, or look at the scale

questions.question: A shop sold 12 ice creams on Monday, 8 on Tuesday, and 15 on Wednesday. How many ice creams were sold in these three days?
questions.explanation: Add the ice creams sold each day: 12 + 8 + 15 = 35 ice creams in total.

commonMistakes.mistake: Added only two of the three numbers
commonMistakes.conceptGap: Forgetting to include all values when finding a total
commonMistakes.remediation: Write down all the numbers first, then add them one by one

questions.question: In a survey, 6 children chose red, 9 chose blue, 3 chose yellow, and 7 chose green as their favourite colour. How many more children chose blue than yellow?
questions.explanation: Blue was chosen by 9 children and yellow by 3 children. The difference is 9 - 3 = 6. So 6 more children chose blue than yellow.

commonMistakes.mistake: Gave the number who chose blue instead of the difference
commonMistakes.conceptGap: Confusing the total for a category with the difference between two categories
commonMistakes.remediation: When asked 'how many more', subtract the smaller number from the bigger number

excellent.message: Wonderful! You are great at counting, comparing, and reading numbers. You are all set to learn about data handling, pictographs, and bar graphs!

good.message: Great job! You know how to count, sort, and compare really well. Let's learn how to show data using tally marks, pictographs, and bar graphs!

partial.message: You know some basics, but let's review a few things first. Watch the review videos about counting, comparing, and reading numbers. Then you will be all set!

needs_review.message: No worries! Let's first watch some fun videos about counting, sorting, and comparing. Then you will be ready to learn how to make cool graphs and charts!

feedbackMessages.on_completion: Great job completing the readiness check!
feedbackMessages.low_score_encouragement: Don't worry! We have some fun videos to help you get ready. Data handling is like detective work and you are going to love it!
feedbackMessages.high_score_praise: Awesome! You are a counting and comparing champion! Let's learn to make amazing graphs and charts!
feedbackMessages.retake_option: After watching the recommended videos, you can try again to check your progress.
